quotes.verbatim_quote:
The first scale is used to solo over the first four bars of Autumn Leaves, Am7-D7-
Gmaj7-Cmaj7, as all chords are in the key of G major.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This establishes the explicit chord-context rule for deploying the major blues scale, grounding the scale in its functional harmonic home.

quotes.verbatim_quote: The b3 is the “blue” note in this scale, as it gives the scale its bluesy sound.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This identifies the single chromatic pitch that defines the character of the major blues scale and distinguishes it from the major pentatonic.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
You can use this scale to solo over any progression in a major key, such as Autumn
Leaves, or over individual 7th and maj7 chords. Because there is not 7th in this
scale, it can be applied to both G7 and Gmaj7 chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This gives the student an explicit avoidance-of-clash rule explaining why the scale is safe over both dominant and major seventh chords.

quotes.topic: Natural Minor over minor ii chord
quotes.verbatim_quote:
The next scale is used to solo over the F#m7b5 chord in Autumn Leaves.

Because minor ii
quotes.why_load_bearing: This introduces the shared-root strategy for navigating a minor ii–V–I, a fundamental organisational concept for jazz soloing that reduces fingering complexity.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This scale is the 6th mode of the major scale, and is also called the Aeolian mode:
quotes.why_load_bearing: This cross-references the natural minor scale to its modal identity, giving students a conceptual bridge to broader modal vocabulary.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
When soloing over the V7alt chord (the B7alt chord in Autumn Leaves) in a minor
ii V I, you use the tonic harmonic minor scale.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This states the core rule for handling the altered dominant in a minor ii–V–I, directing the student to the tonic harmonic minor rather than the chord’s own root.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Here are the notes of the harmonic minor scale. Note that the only difference
with the natural minor scale is the natural 7 instead of a flat 7:
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the harmonic minor by its single differentiating interval, making it easy for students to derive it from the already-learned natural minor.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
When playing the E harmonic minor scal over B7alt, you create a V7(b9,b13)
sound:
quotes.why_load_bearing: This names the specific altered tensions produced when harmonic minor is superimposed over the V7alt chord, connecting scale choice to chord-tone vocabulary.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The final scale is used to solo over the Im7 chord in a minor ii V |, melodic minor.

This scale has a raised 7th interval, which creates tension over m7 chords in your
solos:
quotes.why_load_bearing: This assigns melodic minor its harmonic function in the minor ii–V–I and flags the raised 7th as an expressive tension device rather than a neutral colour tone.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Because of this tension, experiment with this scale in your solos to determine
where you want to use that raised 7th in your lines.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This gives a deliberate-choice rule, instructing students to treat the raised 7th as a context-dependent decision rather than an automatic inclusion.
This aphorism encapsulates the chapter’s governing principle—that scale selection is driven by chord-by-chord harmonic context—and serves as the conceptual capstone of the entire section.
